title: Coupon Collection

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A box contains one of N distinct coupon types, uniformly at random. You buy boxes one at a time until you have at least one of each type.
rules: Coupon types are i.i.d. uniform over {1, 2, ..., N}.
Part A: How many boxes on average?
Part B: After exactly n boxes (n possibly less than what's needed), what's the expected number of DISTINCT types collected?
setup.question: Find E[X] for both questions.

learningObjectives: Decompose total trips as a sum of geometric waiting times.
Use E[Geometric(p)] = 1/p to sum: N/N + N/(N-1) + ... + N/1 = N * H_N.
For Part B, use indicators I_i = 1 if type i appears at least once.

stages.label: Part A: decompose into stages
stages.prompt: Let X_i = number of additional coupons needed to get the i-th NEW type, GIVEN i - 1 types are already collected. What's the distribution of X_i?

options.text: Geometric with success probability (N - i + 1) / N.  E[X_i] = N / (N - i + 1).

stages.explanation: After collecting i - 1 types, each new coupon is 'new' with probability (N - i + 1)/N. Wait time to first success is Geometric. Expected wait: 1/p = N/(N - i + 1).

stages.label: Part A: sum the expected waits
stages.prompt: E[total] = E[X_1] + E[X_2] + ... + E[X_N] = sum from i=1 to N of N/(N - i + 1). Simplify.

options.text: = N * (1/N + 1/(N-1) + ... + 1/1) = N * H_N (the N-th harmonic number).

stages.explanation: Re-index: as i runs 1..N, (N - i + 1) runs N..1. So sum = N * (1/N + 1/(N-1) + ... + 1/1) = N * H_N. Asymptotically H_N ~ ln(N) + gamma.

stages.label: Part A: compute for N = 10
stages.prompt: E[trips to collect all 10 types] = 10 * H_10 = 10 * (1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/10). Compute.
stages.explanation: H_10 = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/10 ≈ 2.929. So 10 * H_10 ≈ 29.29. To collect 10 types you need ~29 boxes on average.

stages.label: Part B: expected distinct types after n boxes
stages.prompt: Let Y = number of distinct types after n boxes. Decompose using indicators: Y = sum of I(type i appears at least once). E[Y] = sum of P(type i in at least one of n boxes) = ?
stages.explanation: P(type i missed in one box) = (N - 1)/N. P(type i missed in n boxes) = ((N - 1)/N)^n. So P(type i appears) = 1 - ((N-1)/N)^n. Sum N indicators.

stages.label: Part B: compute for N = 10, n = 20
stages.prompt: E[Y] for N = 10 types and n = 20 boxes = 10 * (1 - 0.9^20). Compute to 4 decimals.
stages.explanation: 0.9^20 ≈ 0.1216. So 10 * (1 - 0.1216) = 10 * 0.8784 ≈ 8.78. After 20 boxes you typically have around 8-9 of the 10 types.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: How many boxes on average for N = 365 (one of each day-of-year)?
Asymptotically, E[trips] ~ N * ln(N). What's the variance? (It's known to scale as N^2 * pi^2 / 6.)
Why does the SUM of geometric waits give the answer? What if we wanted a specific TYPE first?
